panel.origin: A bright, tart ester profile linked to specific oxidation levels, commonly found in high-grade Oriental Beauty or Dan Cong Oolongs.
panel.mixology: A zesty, bright red fruit flavor that gives the tea a lively and refreshing tartness.
panel.examples: Bug-bitten teas (like Bai Hao Oolong) naturally develop a raspberry/honey flavor as the plant's defense mechanism releases unique terpenes.

panel.origin: Sweet, jammy aromatic lactones and furaneol notes. Found in lightly oxidized black teas from high elevations.
panel.mixology: A sweet, soft, and slightly juicy fruitiness, similar to ripe strawberries or strawberry jam.
panel.examples: First flush Darjeelings can sometimes express delicate strawberry nuances due to the hard wither process.

panel.origin: Dark fruit esters and mild tannins interacting to create a deep, slightly tart profile. Common in robust Assams or Kenyan black teas.
panel.mixology: A rich, dark berry flavor with a satisfyingly thick, slightly tart finish.
panel.examples: Blackberry leaves themselves are often used in herbal blends to mimic tea-like tannins.

panel.origin: P-mentha-8-thiol-3-one. A distinctly tart, slightly woody and catty aromatic characteristic of some Kenyan and Rwandan black teas.
panel.mixology: A sharp, vibrant dark berry flavor, famously associated with traditional British black tea blends.
panel.examples: Black currant is the signature flavor of many classic Earl Grey variations to add a fruity depth to the bergamot.

panel.origin: Limonene and geraniol interactions. Frequently noted in specific Ceylon (Sri Lankan) teas grown at high altitudes.
panel.mixology: A sweet, bright, and universally pleasant citrus flavor that adds sunshine to the cup.
panel.examples: Orange Pekoe is a grade of tea leaf, not an orange flavor! It denotes a whole leaf size.

panel.origin: Citral and limonene compounds. Offers a bright astringency. Often an added ingredient, but naturally occurring in some high-mountain green teas.
panel.mixology: A crisp, sharply acidic citrus note that makes the tea feel highly cleansing and refreshing.
panel.examples: Adding actual lemon to tea lightens its color because the citric acid alters the pH, affecting the tea's tannins.

panel.origin: Nootkatone and mercaptan compounds. A bitter-citrus profile occasionally found in highly oxidized, high-altitude oolongs.
panel.mixology: A sharp, slightly bitter, and deeply aromatic citrus flavor, like pink grapefruit peel.
panel.examples: Some modern tea cultivars are specifically bred to express higher levels of grapefruit-like terpenes.

panel.origin: Concentrated essential oils (terpenes) without the citric acid bite. Highly fragrant, found in heavily withered white and oolong teas.
panel.mixology: The aromatic, perfumey scent of a freshly grated lemon or orange peel, without the sour juice.
panel.examples: Earl Grey is the most famous zesty tea, flavored with the essential oil extracted from the rind of the bergamot orange.

panel.origin: Malic acid and hexanal compounds. A crisp, slightly astringent note prominent in young Sheng (Raw) Pu-erh.
panel.mixology: A tart, mouth-watering crispness similar to biting into a Granny Smith apple.
panel.examples: Sheng Pu-erh often starts its aging life with strong green apple notes that transition into dark plum or leather over decades.

panel.origin: Mellow esters and oxidation byproducts. A soft, sweet fruitiness common in Keemun black teas.
panel.mixology: A soft, sweet, and comforting fruit flavor, like fresh apple cider.
panel.examples: Keemun, a famous Chinese black tea, is historically celebrated for its distinct apple and orchid profile.

panel.origin: Isoamyl acetate. A fermentation byproduct often found in heavily roasted or uniquely processed dark oolongs.
panel.mixology: A dense, sweet, and creamy fruit flavor similar to banana bread.
panel.examples: Banana notes are quite rare in pure tea but can emerge when tea leaves undergo a heavy 'kill-green' (sha qing) phase at specific temperatures.

panel.origin: Lactones (gamma-decalactone). A signature note of high-end Dan Cong oolongs (like Mi Lan Xiang) and first-flush Darjeeling.
panel.mixology: A lush, fuzzy, and sweet stone-fruit flavor that is highly prized in fine teas.
panel.examples: Mi Lan Xiang literally translates to 'Honey Orchid Fragrance', but to Western palates, it overwhelmingly tastes of ripe peach.

panel.origin: A concentrated, slightly tart lactone profile. Characteristic of heavily withered white teas like Bai Mu Dan (White Peony).
panel.mixology: A sweet yet slightly tangy flavor, reminiscent of dried apricots or apricot jam.
panel.examples: The fuzzy down on silver needle white teas helps protect the delicate aromatic oils that produce these apricot notes.

panel.origin: Subtle ethyl acetate expressions at low concentrations. Found in delicate green teas and light oolongs like Baozhong.
panel.mixology: A mild, crisp, and elegantly sweet flavor, much like an Asian pear.
panel.examples: Baozhong oolong from Taiwan is famous for its ethereal, pear-like aromatics.

panel.origin: Concentrated sugars and Maillard reaction products. Prominent in aged teas and heavily roasted Wuyi rock oolongs.
panel.mixology: A deep, rich, and concentrated sweetness like raisins, prunes, or figs.
panel.examples: The 'dried fruit' note in Wuyi Rock Teas is often a result of slow, traditional charcoal roasting.

panel.origin: Nonadienal compounds. A refreshing, watery-sweet note found in high-grade Japanese Sencha and Gyokuro.
panel.mixology: A cool, sweet, and highly refreshing flavor similar to honeydew or cantaloupe.
panel.examples: Melon notes in Japanese green teas are often enhanced by 'shading' the tea plants before harvest.

panel.origin: Complex floral-fruity esters. Common in Taiwanese black teas (like Ruby #18) and certain Dan Congs.
panel.mixology: A sweet, floral, and intensely exotic fruit flavor unique to Southeast Asia.
panel.examples: Ruby #18 is a unique hybrid cultivar created in Taiwan specifically to produce notes of mint, cinnamon, and lychee.

panel.origin: A sharp, sweet-tart ester profile. Can emerge in heavily oxidized, bug-bitten Taiwanese teas.
panel.mixology: A bright, intensely sweet and tangy tropical fruit flavor.
panel.examples: In the tea world, pineapple notes are a sign of a highly active oxidation phase where enzymes rapidly break down catechins.

panel.origin: Jasmone and linalool. Historically added via scenting, but can naturally occur in lightly oxidized Tieguanyin.
panel.mixology: An intoxicatingly sweet, highly aromatic, and elegant flower scent.
panel.examples: Authentic jasmine tea isn't flavored with oils; it's made by repeatedly layering green tea leaves with night-blooming jasmine flowers.

panel.origin: A delicate, slightly powdery floral terpene profile found in certain high-mountain Taiwanese oolongs.
panel.mixology: A soft, spring-like floral note that feels light and slightly sweet.
panel.examples: Floral notes in oolongs are developed by carefully bruising the edges of the tea leaves during the withering process.

panel.origin: Complex aromatic profile (linalool, geraniol, nerolidol). The holy grail of Tieguanyin and Dan Cong oolongs.
panel.mixology: A rich, sophisticated, and pervasive floral flavor that lingers long after swallowing.
panel.examples: Tieguanyin (Iron Goddess of Mercy) is historically judged by the strength and persistence of its natural orchid aroma (Yun).

panel.origin: A sweet, nectar-like floral note. Highly associated with high-grade Silver Needle (Bai Hao Yinzhen) white tea.
panel.mixology: A nostalgic, sweet, and delicate floral flavor like sucking the nectar from a honeysuckle blossom.
panel.examples: Honeysuckle notes are most prominent in teas that are primarily composed of unopened leaf buds.

panel.origin: Coumarin compounds. A slightly sweet, almond-like floral note found in some Japanese green teas, particularly Sakura-scented blends.
panel.mixology: A gentle, slightly sweet and nutty floral flavor reminiscent of Japanese spring.
panel.examples: In Japan, Sakura (cherry blossom) tea is traditionally served at weddings as a symbol of new beginnings.

panel.origin: Neroli-like aromatics. Can naturally occur in lightly oxidized, high-elevation Ceylon teas.
panel.mixology: A bright, zesty, and highly perfumed floral scent with a hint of citrus.
panel.examples: Orange blossom notes are highly volatile, meaning you will smell them strongly but they may fade quickly on the palate.

panel.origin: Geraniol and citronellol. Often blended, but naturally expressed in high-grade Keemun black tea.
panel.mixology: A deep, romantic, and velvety floral flavor that pairs beautifully with dark teas.
panel.examples: Rose Congou is a classic Chinese tea where black tea is layered with actual rose petals during processing.

panel.origin: A slightly bitter, yellow-floral, and earthy note found in young, raw Pu-erh teas.
panel.mixology: A rustic, slightly earthy and sunny floral flavor.
panel.examples: Roasted dandelion root is often used as a caffeine-free coffee substitute, sharing some earthy profiles with dark teas.

panel.origin: Ionone compounds. A sweet, powdery floral note occasionally found in Darjeeling second flush teas.
panel.mixology: A very sweet, candy-like floral note similar to vintage violet pastilles.
panel.examples: The 'muscatel' character of Darjeeling tea often carries subtle undertones of violet.

panel.origin: A sharp, slightly green and citrusy floral note. Driven by geraniol. Common in Dan Cong oolongs.
panel.mixology: A pungent, lively floral flavor with a slightly herbal or citrus edge.
panel.examples: Geraniol is one of the primary aromatic compounds naturally produced by the tea plant (Camellia sinensis).

panel.origin: Humulene and myrcene. A bitter, floral-resinous note sometimes found in heavily oxidized Indian black teas.
panel.mixology: A sharp, slightly bitter floral note similar to the aroma of an IPA beer.
panel.examples: Hops and tea both rely heavily on terpenes for their complex aromatic profiles.

panel.origin: An overwhelming, complex blend of esters and terpenes. Can be a sign of artificial flavoring if unbalanced.
panel.mixology: A highly concentrated, aromatic experience that smells almost like a cosmetic or perfume.
panel.examples: When tasting tea professionally, cuppers slurp the tea aggressively to atomize these perfumey oils into the nasal cavity.

panel.origin: Benzaldehyde. A sweet, slightly fruity nutty note found in Longjing (Dragon Well) green tea.
panel.mixology: A smooth, sweet, and slightly dessert-like nuttiness similar to marzipan.
panel.examples: Longjing tea is famously pan-fired in giant woks, pressing the leaves flat and developing this signature roasted almond flavor.

panel.origin: Pyrazines developed through roasting. Common in lower-grade roasted green teas or Genmaicha.
panel.mixology: A rich, savory, and oily nuttiness exactly like freshly roasted peanuts.
panel.examples: In Taiwan, some roasted oolongs are intentionally baked for days to develop deep peanut and caramel notes.

panel.origin: Slightly astringent, tannic, and earthy nuttiness. Found in some Yunnan black teas (Dianhong).
panel.mixology: A robust, slightly dry nuttiness with an earthy finish, similar to walnut skins.
panel.examples: The tannins in walnut skins are very similar chemically to the catechins found in tea leaves.

panel.origin: A sweet, starchy, and roasted profile. The absolute hallmark of high-quality Chinese green teas (like Bi Luo Chun).
panel.mixology: A warm, sweet, and comforting flavor identical to roasted chestnuts in the winter.
panel.examples: To achieve the chestnut flavor, Chinese green teas are typically pan-fired, whereas Japanese green teas are steamed (yielding grassy notes).

panel.origin: A rich, sweet, and woody nuttiness. Present in some roasted Wuyi Rock teas.
panel.mixology: A highly aromatic, sweet, and creamy nut flavor, reminiscent of praline.
panel.examples: Hazelnut notes in rock tea (Yancha) are a direct result of the Maillard reaction during the careful charcoal roasting process.

panel.origin: A general pyrazine presence from high-heat processing (roasting or firing).
panel.mixology: A deeply savory, warm, and comforting aroma like walking past a roasted nut cart.
panel.examples: Hojicha, a Japanese roasted green tea, is entirely defined by this roasted nut and caramel profile.

panel.origin: Phenylacetaldehyde. A rich, viscous sweetness. Very common in bug-bitten teas like Oriental Beauty.
panel.mixology: A thick, floral, and lingering sweetness that coats the mouth.
panel.examples: When leafhoppers bite tea leaves, the plant releases chemicals that, once processed, turn into a distinct honey flavor.

panel.origin: Sotolon compounds. A woody, rich sweetness found in oxidized, high-altitude black teas.
panel.mixology: A comforting, rich, and earthy sweetness identical to real maple syrup.
panel.examples: Some black teas from the high mountains of Taiwan naturally taste so much like maple syrup it feels like they were sweetened.

panel.origin: Maltol. The defining characteristic of Assam black tea. A starchy, bready sweetness.
panel.mixology: A hearty, robust, and bready sweetness, similar to malted milk balls or a rich dark beer.
panel.examples: Malty Assam tea is the foundational ingredient in most Irish Breakfast blends due to its ability to stand up to milk.

panel.origin: A combination of high unfermented sugars and floral esters. Common in silver needle white tea.
panel.mixology: A very light, delicate, and floral sweetness, like a drop of sugar water from a flower.
panel.examples: White teas are the least processed of all teas, allowing the natural nectar-like sweetness of the leaf bud to shine.

panel.origin: Caramelized plant sugars from roasting. A signature note of dark roasted oolongs and Hojicha.
panel.mixology: A deep, rich, and slightly burnt sugar flavor that feels dessert-like.
panel.examples: Roasting tea at high temperatures converts the amino acids and sugars into complex caramel compounds via the Maillard reaction.

panel.origin: Dark, slightly bitter, concentrated sweetness. Found in aged Ripe (Shou) Pu-erh.
panel.mixology: A thick, dark, and slightly earthy sweetness, like brown sugar or treacle.
panel.examples: Shou Pu-erh undergoes a 'wet piling' fermentation process that completely transforms its sugars into dark molasses notes.

panel.origin: Advanced caramelization. Often found in heavily baked Wuyi Rock teas (Yancha).
panel.mixology: A sharp, intense sweetness with a slightly bitter, smoky edge, like the top of a crème brûlée.
panel.examples: Wuyi Rock teas are traditionally roasted over charcoal ash for hours to develop this specific burnt sugar profile.

panel.origin: A highly volatile, simple sweet ester profile, often fading quickly. Sometimes found in fresh green teas.
panel.mixology: A bright, simple, and slightly artificial-seeming sweetness, like a hard candy.
panel.examples: In professional tasting, a 'candy' sweetness is sometimes considered less sophisticated than a complex 'honey' or 'malt' sweetness.

panel.origin: Theobromine and pyrazines. A dry, slightly bitter, brown spice note. Found in high-grade Golden Monkey or Jin Jun Mei black teas.
panel.mixology: A smooth, slightly dry, and rich flavor similar to unsweetened cocoa powder.
panel.examples: Tea contains theobromine, the exact same mildly stimulating alkaloid that makes chocolate feel so good to eat.

panel.origin: Eugenol. A sharp, medicinal spice note. Can occur in heavily oxidized wild arbor black teas.
panel.mixology: A pungent, warm, and highly aromatic baking spice with a slight numbing effect.
panel.examples: Eugenol is a naturally occurring compound in both tea and cloves, giving them their shared spicy bite.

panel.origin: Thymol. A savory, herbal spice note occasionally found in Mediterranean-grown or wild teas.
panel.mixology: A savory, dry, and slightly woody herbal flavor.
panel.examples: Herbal notes in true tea are often a reflection of the terroir—the surrounding vegetation and soil composition.

panel.origin: A green, slightly bitter, and fresh spice note. Can be found in under-processed or very young green teas.
panel.mixology: A very fresh, green, and slightly grassy herbal flavor.
panel.examples: If a green tea tastes too much like parsley or raw vegetables, the 'kill-green' heat step may have been too brief.

panel.origin: Myristicin. A warm, sweet, and woody spice note found in some Darjeeling autumnal flushes.
panel.mixology: A sweet, warm, and comforting baking spice.
panel.examples: Autumnal flush teas (harvested in the fall) are famous for their darker, spicier, and more robust profiles.

panel.origin: A cooling, mint-like sensation driven by specific terpenes. A famous characteristic of Taiwanese Ruby #18.
panel.mixology: A distinct cooling sensation in the mouth and throat, similar to peppermint.
panel.examples: The menthol note in Ruby #18 is genetic; it's a crossbreed between a Burmese large-leaf varietal and a wild Taiwanese tea.

panel.origin: Vanillin. A sweet, creamy spice note naturally occurring in aged white teas and some Taiwanese oolongs.
panel.mixology: A smooth, rich, and comforting dessert spice.
panel.examples: As white tea ages (over 3-5 years), its enzymes slowly break down, creating a pronounced natural vanilla flavor.

panel.origin: Piperine-like pungency. Found in robust, thick black teas like Kenyan or Assam.
panel.mixology: A sharp, biting spice that leaves a lingering warmth on the palate.
panel.examples: Chai blends heavily utilize black pepper to cut through the milk and sugar, complementing the tea's natural tannins.

panel.origin: A milder, more earthy and musty spice note compared to black pepper. Found in some aged Sheng Pu-erhs.
panel.mixology: A subtle, earthy spice with a gentle heat.
panel.examples: Aging tea in humid environments (like Hong Kong or Guangdong) often imparts earthy, white-pepper nuances.

panel.origin: Linalool. A citrusy, floral spice note. Very common in high-elevation Sri Lankan (Ceylon) teas.
panel.mixology: A bright, slightly citrusy and nutty spice flavor.
panel.examples: Linalool is a major aromatic compound in tea, giving it both floral (orchid) and spicy (coriander) characteristics.

panel.origin: Cineole. A sweet, pungent, and highly aromatic spice. Often an inclusion, but natural hints exist in some wild Indian teas.
panel.mixology: A complex, sweet, and highly fragrant spice with hints of lemon and mint.
panel.examples: Cardamom is the absolute cornerstone spice of traditional Indian Masala Chai.

panel.origin: Eucalyptol. A sharp, medicinal, and cooling note. Characteristic of some wild Australian or specifically processed dark teas.
panel.mixology: A highly medicinal, clearing, and cooling herbal flavor.
panel.examples: Tea plants absorb aromatics from their environment; teas grown near eucalyptus forests can take on these traits.

panel.origin: Cinnamaldehyde. A sweet, woody spice. The defining characteristic of Rou Gui (Cassia) Wuyi Rock Oolong.
panel.mixology: A warm, sweet, and pervasive baking spice.
panel.examples: Rou Gui is a famous rock tea whose name literally translates to 'Cinnamon', prized for its natural, intense cinnamon aroma.

panel.origin: Glycyrrhizin-like sweetness (Hui Gan). A sweet, lingering aftertaste highly prized in raw Pu-erh.
panel.mixology: A sweet, root-like flavor that lingers in the throat long after swallowing.
panel.examples: In Chinese tea culture, 'Hui Gan' is a returning sweetness in the throat, often compared to the lingering sweetness of licorice root.

panel.origin: Safranal. A slightly metallic, floral, and honeyed spice note. Rare, found in some high-grade Persian or Indian black teas.
panel.mixology: A highly exotic, slightly earthy and sweet floral spice.
panel.examples: True saffron is the most expensive spice in the world, sometimes brewed on its own as a luxury tisane.

panel.origin: Anethole. A sweet, anise-like, and slightly green spice note.
panel.mixology: A sweet, herbal flavor very similar to mild black licorice.
panel.examples: Fennel seeds are commonly used in Ayurvedic tea blends for their reputed digestive benefits.

panel.origin: A dry, particulate carbon note resulting from over-roasting or improper firing.
panel.mixology: A dry, dusty flavor similar to a cold campfire.
panel.examples: While usually a defect, a slight ashy note is expected in traditional, heavily roasted Tieguanyin.

panel.origin: Heavy phenolic compounds from intense smoke exposure. The defining note of Lapsang Souchong.
panel.mixology: An intense, heavy, and slightly medicinal smoky flavor, like fresh asphalt or pine pitch.
panel.examples: Lapsang Souchong is traditionally smoked over smoldering pinewood fires in the Wuyi Mountains.

panel.origin: Guaiacol and syringol. Injected via pine smoke or charcoal roasting.
panel.mixology: A distinct campfire or smoked meat aroma.
panel.examples: Legend has it that smoked tea was invented accidentally when an army camped in a tea factory, forcing the farmers to dry the leaves quickly over pine fires.

panel.origin: Maillard reaction products (furans). A comforting, bready, and roasted profile.
panel.mixology: A warm, comforting flavor exactly like a slice of browned toast.
panel.examples: Toasty notes are the primary reason Genmaicha (green tea with toasted rice) is so beloved as a comfort drink.

panel.origin: A complex, earthy, sweet, and slightly leathery note. Very common in aged raw Pu-erh and some Keemun blacks.
panel.mixology: A rich, sweet, and earthy aroma like unlit pipe tobacco or a cigar humidor.
panel.examples: As raw Pu-erh ages over decades, its sharp astringency mellows out into deep, complex tobacco and leather notes.

panel.origin: Savory pyrazines combined with smoke phenols. Present in some heavy charcoal-roasted oolongs.
panel.mixology: A savory, slightly smoky umami flavor.
panel.examples: Traditional charcoal roasting requires a master roaster to monitor the ash continuously for days to prevent the tea from catching fire.

panel.origin: A severe roasting defect where the leaf carbonizes, creating acrid, bitter flavors.
panel.mixology: An unpleasant, harsh bitterness like scraping the burnt bottom of a pan.
panel.examples: Carbonized tea leaves will feel brittle and powdery to the touch, a clear sign of poor processing.

panel.origin: A mellow, integrated smoke and wood profile, typical of aged Lapsang Souchong.
panel.mixology: A comforting, woody, and gently smoky aroma like sitting by a winter hearth.
panel.examples: High-quality smoked teas balance the smoke so it doesn't overpower the natural floral and fruity notes of the tea leaf.

panel.origin: Hexanal and leaf alcohol. The quintessential profile of steamed Japanese green teas.
panel.mixology: A fresh, bright, and slightly astringent green flavor.
panel.examples: Steaming tea leaves (Japanese style) immediately halts oxidation, preserving the vibrant green color and grassy flavors.

panel.origin: Intense, volatile cis-3-hexenol. A highly vibrant, almost pungent green note.
panel.mixology: The exact, vivid smell of a lawnmower on a summer day.
panel.examples: This compound is actually a distress signal released by the plant when its leaves are broken or cut.

panel.origin: A slightly dry, starchy, and mild green note.
panel.mixology: A dry, earthy, and mild green flavor, similar to chewing on a wheat stalk.
panel.examples: Some lower-grade green teas have a flatter, seed-like profile due to later harvesting times.

panel.origin: A funky, earthy, and slightly animalic note. Often a sign of humid storage or specific organic fertilizers.
panel.mixology: A rustic, funky, and earthy aroma reminiscent of hay and horses.
panel.examples: In the Pu-erh world, a slight 'barnyard' funk is sometimes appreciated as a sign of traditional wet storage (Hou Qi).

panel.origin: A woody, slightly sweet, and pale green profile. Characteristic of Kukicha (twig tea).
panel.mixology: A light, slightly woody and sweet green flavor without the bitterness of leaves.
panel.examples: Kukicha is made entirely of the stems and twigs of the tea plant, making it naturally very low in caffeine.

panel.origin: A dry, oxidized green note. Often found in older or faded green teas (past crop).
panel.mixology: A dry, flat, and dusty green flavor like dried hay.
panel.examples: Green tea has the shortest shelf life of all teas; within a year, its vibrant grassy notes fade into dull straw.

panel.origin: Alpha-pinene. A sharp, resinous wood note. Central to Lapsang Souchong's smoking process.
panel.mixology: A sharp, evergreen aroma like walking through a pine forest.
panel.examples: Only specifically sourced, resin-heavy pine wood is used to traditionally smoke authentic Wuyi Lapsang Souchong.

panel.origin: A sweet, aromatic, and dry wood note. Found in some aged Taiwanese oolongs.
panel.mixology: A sweet, fragrant, and comforting woodiness, like an old cedar chest.
panel.examples: Storing tea in cedar boxes is generally avoided as the strong wood oils will permanently alter the tea's flavor.

panel.origin: A sweet, slightly fruity, and warm wood profile.
panel.mixology: A soft, sweet wood flavor with a hint of red fruit.
panel.examples: Tea caddies in Japan are often crafted from cherry bark (Kabazaiku) because it naturally regulates humidity.

panel.origin: A sappy, astringent, and unripe wood note. Can indicate harsh processing or young leaves.
panel.mixology: A sharp, sappy, and slightly bitter flavor like stripping the bark off a live branch.
panel.examples: A green wood flavor is usually considered a defect, indicating the tea wasn't roasted or oxidized quite enough.

panel.origin: A heavy, rich, and oxidized profile. Common in fully oxidized, older black teas.
panel.mixology: A deep, rich, and antique wood flavor like polished mahogany.
panel.examples: Aged black teas lose their brisk astringency and develop deep, comforting dark wood characteristics.

panel.origin: A dry, particulate, and slightly stale wood note. Often a sign of staling or poor storage.
panel.mixology: A dry, flat, and dusty flavor.
panel.examples: Tea bags often contain 'fannings' and 'dust'—the smallest particles of tea that can sometimes taste like sawdust due to rapid oxidation.

panel.origin: A cooling, medicinal, and earthy note. Highly prized in aged raw Pu-erh from old trees.
panel.mixology: A sharp, cooling, and slightly medicinal flavor similar to Vicks VapoRub or mothballs.
panel.examples: Camphor notes in Pu-erh are believed to come from the tea trees growing alongside actual camphor trees in the Yunnan forests.

panel.origin: A sticky, sharp, and sap-like note. Found in some wild arbor teas.
panel.mixology: A sharp, slightly bitter, and sticky pine-sap flavor.
panel.examples: Tea leaves are coated in a natural wax to protect them; intense processing can sometimes bring out resinous qualities.

panel.origin: A dry, tannic, and earthy profile.
panel.mixology: A dry, rough, and earthy flavor.
panel.examples: Some herbal teas, like Cinnamon or Yohimbe, are actually made entirely of tree bark.

panel.origin: A dense, smoky, and earthy note. Found in heavily roasted or uniquely aged dark teas.
panel.mixology: A rich, earthy, and smoky flavor similar to Islay Scotch whisky.
panel.examples: Peat is rarely used in tea processing, but the flavor profile can be replicated through specific fungal fermentation in dark teas.

panel.origin: A damp, green, and earthy note. Characteristic of some wet-stored Pu-erhs.
panel.mixology: A soft, damp, and earthy flavor like walking through a wet forest.
panel.examples: The environment where tea grows deeply affects its taste; teas from constantly misty, mossy ravines often absorb that character.

panel.origin: A damp, musty profile. Can be a sign of poor storage or a stage in Pu-erh fermentation.
panel.mixology: A damp, heavy flavor like a rain-soaked log.
panel.examples: During the 'wet piling' process of Shou Pu-erh, the tea literally tastes like a wet forest floor for the first few months.

panel.origin: Geosmin. A classic note of ripe (Shou) Pu-erh. Results from microbial fermentation.
panel.mixology: A deep, comforting flavor identical to the smell of soil after a heavy rain (petrichor).
panel.examples: Geosmin, the chemical that causes this flavor, is produced by soil bacteria and is detectable by humans at incredibly low concentrations.

panel.origin: A complex, oxidized, and slightly animalic note. Prized in aged raw Pu-erh and fine Assams.
panel.mixology: A dry, rich, and sophisticated aroma exactly like an old leather armchair or saddle.
panel.examples: As raw Pu-erh ages, its bright, astringent green notes slowly morph into deep leather and tobacco profiles.

panel.origin: A complex blend of damp earth, decaying leaves, and mushrooms. Found in Liu Bao and Pu-erh.
panel.mixology: A savory, damp, and incredibly earthy flavor like autumn leaves on the ground.
panel.examples: Dark teas (Hei Cha) actually rely on specific molds and yeasts to develop this forest floor complexity.

panel.origin: Advanced fungal fermentation. A hallmark of traditional wet-storage dark teas.
panel.mixology: A very heavy, damp, and funky earthiness.
panel.examples: While it sounds unappealing, this note in high-end aged tea provides a thick, comforting mouthfeel that coats the throat.

panel.origin: Extreme fermentation. Often a sign that a ripe Pu-erh hasn't rested long enough after processing.
panel.mixology: An overwhelming, hot, and funky earth aroma.
panel.examples: Newly produced Shou Pu-erh needs to rest for a few years to let these extreme compost notes dissipate.

panel.origin: TCA or damp storage indication. A flat, dull, and dusty note.
panel.mixology: A stale, old basement smell.
panel.examples: Mustiness is generally a defect, meaning the tea was stored in an environment without proper airflow.

panel.origin: Often a sign of hard brewing water or high iron content in the tea leaves.
panel.mixology: A sharp, biting flavor like licking a copper penny.
panel.examples: Historically, cast iron teapots (Tetsubin) were prized because they slightly altered the water's mineral content, sweetening the tea.

panel.origin: A clean, stony, and crisp mineral profile. The absolute signature of Wuyi Rock Teas (Yancha).
panel.mixology: A crisp, clean, and grounding flavor like the smell of river stones after a rain.
panel.examples: Wuyi Rock Teas grow in actual rocky crevices in a national park; their roots absorb immense amounts of minerals, yielding the famous 'Rock Rhyme' (Yan Yun).

panel.origin: A savory, sodium-like impression often tied to high umami/amino acid content.
panel.mixology: A savory, mouth-watering brininess.
panel.examples: In the Tang Dynasty, tea was traditionally boiled with a pinch of salt to enhance its flavor.

panel.origin: Dimethyl sulfide (DMS). A hallmark of heavily shaded Japanese green teas like Gyokuro.
panel.mixology: A rich, savory, and oceanic flavor exactly like nori (sushi seaweed).
panel.examples: Shading Japanese tea plants blocks photosynthesis, which ramps up the production of savory amino acids like L-theanine.

panel.origin: A brisk, slightly briny and fresh ozone note. Found in coastal-grown Japanese Sencha.
panel.mixology: A fresh, breezy, and slightly salty aroma.
panel.examples: Terroir is powerful; tea grown on coastal farms in Japan literally absorbs the salty sea breezes into the leaves.

panel.origin: A severe defect caused by excessive dimethyl sulfide, poor storage, or bad fertilizer.
panel.mixology: An unpleasant, harsh, and fishy smell.
panel.examples: A fishy taste in green tea or matcha is a definitive sign of low quality or improperly applied fish-meal fertilizer.

panel.origin: A sweet, vibrant green note. Highly desirable in premium spring green teas.
panel.mixology: A bright, sweet, and comforting vegetable flavor like fresh snap peas.
panel.examples: The first harvest of spring (Pre-Qingming) yields the sweetest, most pea-like green teas due to high amino acid content built up over winter.

panel.origin: Methoxypyrazines. A sharp, slightly spicy vegetal note.
panel.mixology: A crisp, slightly sharp flavor like a raw green bell pepper.
panel.examples: This note can indicate the tea was grown in a slightly cooler climate, slowing down the breakdown of these specific pyrazines.

panel.origin: A savory, slightly sulfurous green note. Found in some steamed Japanese teas.
panel.mixology: A rich, steamed vegetable flavor.
panel.examples: The savory, asparagus-like umami in fine Japanese tea is so intense it is sometimes compared to chicken broth.

FRUITY.en: Fruity notes — berries, citrus, tree fruit, tropical — are common in fruit-infused or flavored teas, but also occur naturally in high-quality oolongs and black teas through oxidation.
FRUITY.es: Las notas afrutadas — bayas, cítricos, fruta de árbol, tropical — son comunes en los tés con infusión o saborizados, pero también ocurren de forma natural en oolongs y tés negros de alta calidad gracias a la oxidación.

FLORAL.en: Floral notes — jasmine, orchid, rose — are signature of high-grade green and oolong teas, often coming from the tea cultivar itself or from scenting with real flowers.
FLORAL.es: Las notas florales — jazmín, orquídea, rosa — son distintivas de los tés verdes y oolong de alta calidad, y a menudo provienen del propio cultivar del té o de un perfumado con flores reales.

NUTTY.en: Nutty notes — almond, chestnut, roasted nuts — are common in roasted oolongs and some black teas, developing during the roasting or pan-firing step.
NUTTY.es: Las notas a frutos secos — almendra, castaña, frutos secos tostados — son comunes en los oolongs tostados y algunos tés negros, y se desarrollan durante el paso de tostado o cocción en wok.

SWEET.en: Sweet notes — honey, malt, caramel — develop through oxidation (in black teas) or careful withering and roasting, and signal a well-processed, high-quality tea.
SWEET.es: Las notas dulces — miel, malta, caramelo — se desarrollan a través de la oxidación (en los tés negros) o un marchitado y tostado cuidadosos, y señalan un té bien procesado y de alta calidad.

SPICY.en: Spice notes — cinnamon, clove, pepper — are naturally occurring aromatic compounds in the tea plant itself, most pronounced in certain high-mountain and specialty cultivars.
SPICY.es: Las notas de especia — canela, clavo, pimienta — son compuestos aromáticos que ocurren naturalmente en la propia planta del té, y son más pronunciadas en ciertos cultivares de alta montaña y de especialidad.

CHAR.en: Char notes — ash, smoke, tobacco — come from high-heat roasting or, in some traditional styles like Lapsang Souchong, from drying the leaves over a wood fire.
CHAR.es: Las notas a carbonizado — ceniza, humo, tabaco — provienen de un tostado a alta temperatura o, en algunos estilos tradicionales como el Lapsang Souchong, de secar las hojas sobre fuego de leña.

VEGETAL.en: Vegetal notes — grass, wood, earth, mineral, marine, vegetables — are the largest and most diverse group, reflecting everything from fresh green tea (grassy) to aged pu-erh (earthy, woody).
VEGETAL.es: Las notas vegetales — pasto, madera, tierra, mineral, marino, vegetales — son el grupo más grande y diverso, reflejando desde el té verde fresco (herbáceo) hasta el pu-erh añejado (terroso, amaderado).
